description: Understanding disability as social construction, not medical pathology. The social model: disability is not in the body — it is in the environment that fails to accommodate the body. Universal design, crip theory, disability justice.

dome_relevance.1: Legal — ADA, disability rights, guardianship reform
dome_relevance.2: Systems — accessibility of every system, form, portal
dome_relevance.4: Health — disability as identity, not pathology
dome_relevance.10: Autonomy — self-determination, supported decision-making vs guardianship

sphere_relevance.7: Activation — accessible by design, not by retrofit
sphere_relevance.9: Policy — disability policy as design policy

adjacent_disciplines.description: Design for all bodies, all abilities, from the start
Enabling participation in daily life activities

practitioners.name: Judith Butler
practitioners.practice: Performativity theory, vulnerability as political category, precarious life
practitioners.approach: The body is not a given — it is performed, regulated, made legible through repetition. Vulnerability is not weakness — it is the fundamental condition of embodied existence. A politics built on invulnerability is a politics built on denial.
practitioners.body_of_work: Gender Trouble (1990), Bodies That Matter (1993), Precarious Life (2004), Notes Toward a Performative Theory of Assembly (2015). Transformed how we think about bodies, identity, and public space.
practitioners.why_instructive: The dome's layers perform the person into legibility — each layer is a way of reading the body. Butler warns us: the categories we use to help can also constrain. The dome must hold its own categories lightly.

dome_relevance.1: Legal — how law performs and constrains identity categories
dome_relevance.4: Health — how medical categories produce the bodies they claim to describe
dome_relevance.11: Creativity — identity as creative practice, not fixed attribute

sphere_relevance.5: Social — public assembly as political body, vulnerability in shared space
sphere_relevance.7: Activation — gathering as performative act, bodies in space
sphere_relevance.9: Policy — how policy categories produce the populations they govern

practitioners.name: Ed Roberts
practitioners.practice: Independent living movement, disability rights activism, self-determination
practitioners.approach: We are the experts on our own lives. The problem is not the wheelchair — the problem is the stairs. Independent living means disabled people making their own decisions, taking their own risks, living in the community. Not warehoused. Not protected. Free.
practitioners.body_of_work: First severely disabled student at UC Berkeley (1962). Founded the Center for Independent Living (1972) — the first. Became California Director of Rehabilitation. Founded the World Institute on Disability. His insistence on self-determination shaped the ADA.
practitioners.why_instructive: He defined dome layer 10 (Autonomy) for disabled people: the right to take risks, make mistakes, and live freely. The dome must never 'protect' someone out of their own life.

dome_relevance.2: Systems — redesigning service systems around self-determination
dome_relevance.5: Housing — community living over institutional placement
dome_relevance.10: Autonomy — the right to risk, the right to fail, the right to choose

sphere_relevance.2: Infrastructure — accessibility as design principle, not afterthought
sphere_relevance.7: Activation — accessible by design means more people can participate
sphere_relevance.9: Policy — ADA and independent living policy

practitioners.name: Tiny Tim
practitioners.creator: Charles Dickens
practitioners.practice: Being the object of pity that motivates a rich man's reform
practitioners.approach: The disabled child as moral instrument — his suffering exists to teach Scrooge a lesson. His disability is never described medically. His cure is never explained. He exists to make a non-disabled audience feel something.
practitioners.body_of_work: Tiny Tim appears in Stave 3 of A Christmas Carol. His crutch, his fragility, his 'God bless us, everyone' — all designed to soften Scrooge's heart. Without Tim's disability, Scrooge has no motivation to change.
practitioners.why_instructive: He's the anti-pattern — 'inspiration porn.' Using disabled people as motivation for non-disabled people. The dome must never instrumentalize disability. A person's health layer serves THEM, not the system's narrative about redemption.

dome_relevance.4: Health — disability as narrative device rather than lived experience
dome_relevance.10: Autonomy — zero autonomy, exists entirely for others' moral education
